text: An article on Tuesday about four new Ford Motor factories misstated the conditions under which workers at planned Ford Motor factories in Tennessee and Kentucky could come under union jurisdiction. For the United Auto Workers contract to apply at a Ford electric-truck assembly plant in Tennessee, a majority of eligible workers there would need to sign cards backing the union. At three battery plants that would be a joint venture between Ford and another company, unionization efforts would need to start from scratch. The first provision reflects the U.A.W. contract with Ford, and the second reflects federal labor law; neither stems from those states’ right-to-work laws.

text: An article on Page 30 this weekend about the anti-vaccine movement misstates one publication in which Brian Deer’s multipart investigation into Andrew Wakefield’s 1998 Lancet paper was published. It appeared in The BMJ and The Sunday Times of London, but not in The Times of London. The article also misstates the number of segments from Deer’s investigation that appeared in The BMJ; it was five, not two. Finally, the article refers imprecisely to the timing of Deer’s investigation. It began before, not after, 10 of 12 co-authors of the Lancet paper issued a partial retraction in March 2004.

candidates.abstract: A wave of parents has been radicalized by Covid-era misinformation to reject ordinary childhood immunizations — with potentially lethal consequences.

text: An obituary on Friday about O.J. Simpson misstated who owned the Ford Bronco in which he fled after being charged with the murders of Nicole Brown Simpson and Ronald L. Goldman. It was Mr. Simpson’s friend Al Cowlings, the driver — not Mr. Simpson himself. The obituary also misstated what Mr. Simpson’s lawyer Johnnie L. Cochran Jr. told the jury at his murder trial about a glove.
